Maths - Number and operations.skill: Counting review - up to 10
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers counting and recognizing numbers up to 10 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(A)). Students should understand one-to-one correspondence, where each object is counted exactly once. Key concepts include: recognizing number symbols (numerals 0-10), understanding that the last number counted tells 'how many', counting objects in various arrangements (lines, arrays, scattered groups), and comparing small groups to determine which has more or fewer. Students should be able to instantly recognize (subitize) small quantities (1-5) without counting. Use concrete objects, pictures, and number lines as visual supports. Vocabulary: count, number, numeral, more, fewer, same, equal, how many. Common misconceptions include skipping objects when counting, counting an object twice, or not understanding that arrangement doesn't change quantity. Activities should involve hands-on counting with manipulatives, matching numerals to quantities, and real-world counting situations appropriate for 6-7-year-olds.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Count to fill a ten frame
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers using ten frames to count for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(A)). A ten frame is a 2x5 grid that helps students organize counting and develop number sense. Key concepts include filling frames systematically (left to right, top to bottom), recognizing how many dots are shown without counting each one (subitizing), understanding how numbers relate to 5 and 10, and seeing that a full frame equals 10. Vocabulary: ten frame, full, empty, count, how many, more, less, subitize. Common misconceptions include filling frames randomly, miscounting partially filled frames, and not seeing the relationship to 5. Activities should use physical ten frames with counters, matching frames to numerals, and building number sense through repeated ten frame work appropriate for ages 6-7.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Counting tens and ones - up to 20
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers Counting tens and ones - up to 20 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(B)). Students learn to decompose numbers into groups of tens and ones, which is foundational for understanding place value. Key concepts include grouping objects into sets of ten, recognizing that a 'ten' is a bundle of ten ones, and understanding that two-digit numbers are composed of tens and ones. Visual models such as base-ten blocks, ten frames, and place value charts are essential tools. Vocabulary: tens, ones, place value, group, bundle, digit, compose, decompose. Common misconceptions include confusing the tens and ones digits or not understanding that 10 ones equals 1 ten. Activities should include building numbers with manipulatives and writing corresponding equations appropriate for ages 6-7.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Write numbers as tens and ones up to 20
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers writing numbers as tens and ones for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(B)). Students practice expressing numbers in expanded place value form, e.g., 34 = 3 tens and 4 ones. Key concepts include decomposing numbers into place value components, connecting physical models (base-ten blocks) to written representations, and understanding that the same number can be decomposed in multiple ways when regrouping. Vocabulary: tens, ones, write, decompose, expanded form, digit, place value. Common misconceptions include reversing tens and ones digits and not understanding flexible regrouping. Activities should include writing number sentences from block models and converting between forms appropriate for ages 6-7.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Counting tens and ones - up to 99
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers Counting tens and ones - up to 99 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(B)). Students learn to decompose numbers into groups of tens and ones, which is foundational for understanding place value. Visual models such as base-ten blocks, ten frames, and place value charts are essential tools. Vocabulary: tens, ones, place value, group, bundle, digit, compose, decompose. Common misconceptions include confusing the tens and ones digits or not understanding that 10 ones equals 1 ten. Activities should include building numbers with manipulatives and writing corresponding equations appropriate for ages 6-7.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Convert between tens and ones - multiples of ten
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers Convert between tens and ones - multiples of ten for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(B)). Students learn to flexibly convert between different place value representations. Key concepts include understanding that 1 ten equals 10 ones, numbers can be represented in multiple equivalent ways using different groupings, and this flexibility is essential for regrouping in addition and subtraction. Vocabulary: convert, regroup, exchange, tens, ones, hundreds, equivalent, trade. Common misconceptions include thinking there is only one way to represent a number and difficulty understanding that regrouping does not change the total value. Activities should involve trading games with base-ten blocks and solving puzzles requiring flexible place value thinking appropriate for ages 6-7.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Regroup tens and ones - ways to make a number
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers Regroup tens and ones - ways to make a number for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(B)). Regrouping involves exchanging equivalent amounts between place values. Students learn that numbers can be composed and decomposed in multiple ways while maintaining the same total value. For example, 35 can be 3 tens and 5 ones, or 2 tens and 15 ones. Key concepts include understanding that regrouping preserves total value and is foundational for addition with carrying and subtraction with borrowing. Vocabulary: regroup, trade, exchange, compose, decompose, equivalent, bundle. Common misconceptions include believing each number has only one representation and not understanding that regrouping maintains value. Activities should include manipulative-based trading and 'ways to make a number' exercises appropriate for ages 6-7.

Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers Regroup tens and ones for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(B)). Regrouping involves exchanging equivalent amounts between place values. Maths - Number and operations.skill: Write numbers as hundreds, tens, and ones - up to 120
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers Write numbers as hundreds, tens, and ones - up to 120 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(B)). Students extend place value understanding to include hundreds, writing numbers in expanded form showing the value of each digit. For example, 115 = 100 + 10 + 5. Key concepts include identifying the value of each digit based on its position, converting between standard form and expanded form, and understanding that each place value position is ten times the one to its right. Vocabulary: hundreds, tens, ones, expanded form, standard form, place value, digit, value. Common misconceptions include confusing face value with place value and difficulty with numbers containing zero. Activities should include base-ten block building, writing equations, and number form conversion exercises appropriate for ages 6-7.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Counting review - up to 20
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers counting and reviewing numbers up to 20 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(A)). Students extend counting fluency beyond 10, developing familiarity with teen numbers. Key concepts include understanding teen numbers as 'ten and some more,' maintaining one-to-one correspondence with larger sets, counting objects in various arrangements, and connecting counting to numeral recognition for 11-20. Vocabulary: count, teen numbers, eleven through twenty, how many, more, fewer. Common misconceptions include difficulty with teen number names (confusing thirteen/thirty), losing track when counting beyond 10, and not seeing teen numbers as composed of ten and ones. Activities should use double ten frames, counting collections, and numeral matching appropriate for ages 6-7.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Count on ten frames - up to 40
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers counting on ten frames up to 40 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(A)). Students use multiple ten frames to represent and count larger quantities, building understanding of grouping by tens. Key concepts include filling complete ten frames and counting remaining ones, skip counting by tens and then adding ones, understanding that each full frame represents 10, and building toward place value understanding. Vocabulary: ten frame, count, full, tens, ones, how many, skip count. Common misconceptions include miscounting across multiple frames and not recognizing that full frames can be skip-counted by tens. Activities should include physical multi-frame counting, matching frame displays to numbers, and building fluency with quantities to 40 appropriate for ages 6-7.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Counting objects to 100
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers counting objects to 100 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(C)). Students practice counting large collections of objects accurately. Key concepts include organizing objects into groups for efficient counting, using groups of ten, maintaining accurate count with larger sets, and recording the total. Vocabulary: count, objects, group, organize, total, how many, tens, ones. Common misconceptions include losing track with larger collections, double-counting or skipping objects, and not using grouping strategies. Activities should include counting collections of real objects (buttons, beans, cubes), organizing into groups of ten before counting, and recording totals appropriate for ages 6-7. When creating content, build from concrete manipulatives to pictorial representations to abstract notation, ensuring 6-7-year-olds develop both conceptual understanding and procedural fluency.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Number lines - up to 100
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers Number lines - up to 100 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(F)). Number lines are visual tools that represent numbers as points on a line, helping students understand number order, spacing, and relationships. Key concepts include locating and labeling numbers, understanding that numbers increase from left to right, identifying numbers between given points, and using equal intervals. Vocabulary: number line, point, interval, between, before, after, greater than, less than, order. Common misconceptions include not attending to scale, placing numbers without considering equal spacing, and confusion when the line doesn't start at zero. Activities should involve placing numbers on blank number lines, identifying mystery numbers, and using number lines for comparison and ordering tasks appropriate for ages 6-7.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Counting objects to 120
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers counting objects to 120 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(C)). Students extend counting skills to quantities beyond 100, understanding that the number sequence continues past the hundred mark. Key concepts include counting past 100 (101, 102... 120), organizing large collections into groups of ten for efficient counting, and understanding that 120 = 12 tens = 1 hundred and 2 tens. Vocabulary: count, hundred, objects, group, organize, beyond one hundred. Common misconceptions include difficulty transitioning past 100 and not knowing number names beyond 100. Activities should include counting large collections, hundred chart extensions, and real-world counting of large quantities appropriate for ages 6-7.

Key concepts include representing numbers with models, reading models to determine the number shown, and understanding the relationship between ones, tens, and hundreds. Common misconceptions include miscounting blocks, confusing tens rods with ones cubes, and not connecting models to written numbers. Maths - Number and operations.skill: Ten more or less
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers Ten more or less for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.5(C)). Students learn to mentally add or subtract 10 from any given number. Key concepts include understanding that adding 10 increases the tens digit by 1 while the ones digit stays the same, and subtracting 10 decreases the tens digit by 1. On a hundred chart, 10 more is directly below and 10 less is directly above. Vocabulary: ten more, ten less, tens digit, ones digit, pattern, mental math, hundred chart. Common misconceptions include changing the ones digit and difficulty when regrouping is needed (e.g., 10 more than 95). Activities should include hundred chart explorations, base-ten block demonstrations, and mental math games appropriate for ages 6-7.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: One more or less - up to 120
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers One more or less - up to 120 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(D)). Students learn to identify the number that is one more or one less than a given number, connecting to the counting sequence. Key concepts include understanding that one more means the next counting number and one less means the previous one, and recognizing what happens at decade boundaries (e.g., one more than 39 is 40). Vocabulary: one more, one less, next, before, after, counting sequence. Common misconceptions include difficulty at decade transitions and confusing one more with ten more. Activities should use number lines, hundred charts, and concrete objects appropriate for ages 6-7.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Comparing: more or fewer?
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers comparing groups to determine more or fewer for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(E)). Students compare two groups of objects to determine which has more, fewer, or if they have the same amount. Key concepts include matching objects one-to-one between groups, counting each group and comparing the numbers, and using comparison language correctly. Vocabulary: more, fewer, less, same, equal, compare, count, group. Common misconceptions include confusing more and fewer, comparing by visual appearance rather than counting, and difficulty when groups are arranged differently. Activities should include side-by-side group comparisons, matching activities, and real-world comparison situations appropriate for ages 6-7.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Compare numbers up to 10 using words
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers Compare numbers up to 10 using words for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(E)). Students express number comparisons using words such as 'greater than,' 'less than,' and 'equal to.' Key concepts include comparing numbers using place value understanding, expressing comparisons in complete sentences, and understanding that comparison is relative (a number can be greater than one number but less than another). Vocabulary: greater than, less than, equal to, compare, more, fewer, same. Common misconceptions include confusing comparison terms and not using place value to compare systematically. Activities should include comparing with base-ten blocks, writing comparison statements, and sorting numbers from least to greatest appropriate for ages 6-7.

Maths - Number and operations.skill: Use place value to compare numbers up to 100
Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers Use place value to compare numbers up to 100 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(E)). Students apply place value understanding to compare multi-digit numbers systematically. Key concepts include starting comparison from the largest place value, moving to smaller place values only when digits are equal, and understanding that a digit in a higher place value position is worth more than any combination of digits in lower positions. Vocabulary: place value, compare, greater than, less than, equal to, digit, ones, tens, hundreds. Common misconceptions include comparing digits without considering place value, comparing from right to left, and thinking longer numbers are always greater. Activities should include place value chart comparisons, using base-ten blocks to compare, and ordering sets of numbers appropriate for ages 6-7.

Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers Put numbers in order - up to 100 for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(F)). Students arrange sets of numbers from least to greatest or greatest to least using place value understanding. Key concepts include comparing multiple numbers systematically, checking all numbers against each other, understanding ascending vs. descending order, and verifying the final arrangement. Maths - Number and operations.prompt: This topic covers Compare numbers up to 10 using symbols for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.2(G)). Students learn to use mathematical symbols (>, <, =) to express number comparisons. Key concepts include understanding that the open end of the symbol faces the greater number, connecting symbols to verbal expressions (greater than, less than, equal to), and using place value to determine which symbol to use. Students visualize combining tens and ones using base-ten blocks or similar models. Science.teks_description: Record observations and describe basic life cycles of organisms
Science.prompt: This topic covers Basic life cycles (plants, animals) for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.13(B) (Record observations and describe basic life cycles of organisms)). Students learn that living organisms go through predictable growth stages. Key concepts include plant life cycles (seed, sprout, seedling, mature plant, flower/fruit/seed), animal life cycles (birth, growth, reproduction, death), and metamorphosis (complete: egg-larva-pupa-adult; incomplete: egg-nymph-adult). Students should compare life cycles and understand that some young don't resemble their parents. Vocabulary: life cycle, stage, growth, metamorphosis, egg, larva, pupa, adult, seed, seedling. Common misconceptions include thinking all animals undergo metamorphosis. Activities should include observing live organisms, sequencing stages, and comparing across species appropriate for ages 6-7.

Science.teks_description: Collect observations and measurements as evidence (using senses to observe)
Science.prompt: This topic covers Introduction to the five senses for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.1(E) (Collect observations and measurements as evidence (using senses to observe))). Students use their five senses (sight, hearing, touch, smell, taste) as scientific observation tools. Key concepts include identifying which sense provides what information, understanding senses as evidence-gathering tools, and safety limits on taste and smell. Vocabulary: observe, senses, sight, hearing, touch, smell, taste, evidence, property, texture. Common misconceptions include thinking only eyes observe. Activities should include sensory stations, mystery boxes, and science journal recording appropriate for ages 6-7. When generating content for this topic, frame questions and explanations using concrete, observable examples that 6-7-year-olds can relate to from their daily lives.

Science.teks_description: Describe observable weather characteristics and their impacts; 1.9 - Describe and predict seasonal patterns
Science.prompt: This topic covers Exploring weather and seasons for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.10(D) (Describe observable weather characteristics and their impacts; 1.9 - Describe and predict seasonal patterns)). Students learn about daily weather conditions and seasonal patterns. Key concepts include weather elements (temperature, precipitation, wind, clouds), weather vs. seasons, seasonal characteristics, and weather's impact on daily life. Vocabulary: weather, temperature, precipitation, wind, cloud, season, pattern, predict. Common misconceptions include confusing weather with climate and thinking seasons change suddenly. Activities should include daily observations, temperature graphing, and seasonal comparisons appropriate for ages 6-7. When generating content for this topic, frame questions and explanations using concrete, observable examples that 6-7-year-olds can relate to from their daily lives.

Students observe, describe, and classify objects by physical properties: color, shape, size, texture, flexibility, hardness, temperature, magnetism, buoyancy. Vocabulary: property, material, texture, hard, soft, flexible, rigid, transparent, magnetic, float, sink, classify. Common misconceptions include confusing properties with uses. Activities should include sorting, testing, and recording properties appropriate for ages 6-7. When generating content for this topic, frame questions and explanations using concrete, observable examples that 6-7-year-olds can relate to from their daily lives. Science.teks_description: Explain how pushes and pulls can start, stop, or change the speed or direction of an object's motion
Science.prompt: This topic covers Understanding how things move (pushes and pulls) for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.7(A) (Explain how pushes and pulls can start, stop, or change the speed or direction of an object's motion)). Pushes and pulls are forces that cause motion changes. Key concepts include push=away, pull=toward, force strength affects speed/distance, and forces can change direction. Vocabulary: force, push, pull, motion, speed, direction, strength, distance. Common misconceptions include thinking objects move without force. Activities should include hands-on force experiments, ramp investigations, and connecting to everyday experiences appropriate for ages 6-7. When generating content for this topic, frame questions and explanations using concrete, observable examples that 6-7-year-olds can relate to from their daily lives.

Science.teks_description: Describe and predict patterns of seasons of the year (patterns in nature including sky objects)
Science.prompt: This topic covers Basic astronomy (sun, moon, and stars) for Grade 1 students (TEKS 1.9(A) (Describe and predict patterns of seasons of the year (patterns in nature including sky objects))). Students learn about sky objects: the Sun (a star providing light and heat), Moon (reflects sunlight, phases), and stars (distant suns). Key concepts include predictable patterns, Moon phases, and apparent sky movement. Vocabulary: Sun, Moon, star, phase, orbit, rotate, day, night, pattern. Common misconceptions include Moon making its own light and Sun moving around Earth. Activities should include Moon observations, day/night modeling, and phase tracking appropriate for ages 6-7. When generating content for this topic, frame questions and explanations using concrete, observable examples that 6-7-year-olds can relate to from their daily lives.

Science.teks_description: Classify living and nonliving things based upon whether they have basic needs and produce young

Students classify things as living (need food/water/air, grow, reproduce, respond) or nonliving. Vocabulary: living, nonliving, needs, grow, reproduce, respond, organism. Common misconceptions include classifying dead things as nonliving and moving objects as living. Activities should include sorting, observing organisms, and discussing edge cases appropriate for ages 6-7. When generating content for this topic, frame questions and explanations using concrete, observable examples that 6-7-year-olds can relate to from their daily lives. Encourage scientific thinking by asking students to predict outcomes before investigating, describe what they observe using precise vocabulary, and explain their reasoning.

Science.teks_description: Use scientific practices to plan and conduct investigations and engineering practices to design solutions

Students learn scientific investigation: asking questions, predicting, designing tests, collecting data, drawing conclusions. Key concepts include testable questions, fair tests (one variable), and evidence-based conclusions. Vocabulary: question, predict, investigate, observe, record, data, conclusion, fair test, variable. Common misconceptions include thinking experiments need lab equipment. Activities should include guided investigations, science notebooks, and follow-up questioning appropriate for ages 6-7. When generating content for this topic, frame questions and explanations using concrete, observable examples that 6-7-year-olds can relate to from their daily lives.
